One hundred feet frontage or
Pacific avenue has been bought by
the City Dye Works for a plant tc
cost about $4,000. The property
which lies near Garfield street
was purchased from Newton Green
for $4,000.


The record of the hearing of the
Washington Water Power company
Hooded lands case, Just completed
IN Occur dAlene before the in.
terror department, contains a total
OF 100000 words, covering 5000
pages of closely typewriter mat.
ter. This is said to be the heaviest
record ever sent to the interior de.
partment (as the records and ex.
hibits weigh half toni and
involves the right of the Wash
ington Water Power company to
exercise eminent domain.


The Vermont Loan G. Trust COM
pany is planning the erection of a
$4,000 apartment house on Ninth
avenue, just off Mcclellan street.
The plans call for a structure 100
feet on Ninth avenue by 4l0 feet
deep. 1t will be four stories high
| and finished for three and four
room apartments.


M. h. Whitehouse has Just pur-
chased the southwest corner of
Third avenue and Bernard street
from Jacob Woolley for $4,000.


The March rainfall fell below the
average Of 1874 inches during the
last 30 years, only a total of rs
being recorded.


The rentals of offices in the
Hyde block have been greatly in
creased, causing a storm of protest
from the tenants. Office rooms
are in demand and the company
feels that it is entitled to all it can
get.


At the branch U. S. land office
in this city at the opening of the
Spokane Indian reservation yester-
day only 10 responded. In the alb
sence of Allen Newton of Helena,
Mont, holder of No. /, Miss Mabel
d. McNickle of Chicago had first
choice and filed on 1873 acres in the
northeast part of the reservation.
Others who filed were A. j. Snoddy
of Miller, S. d., holder of No. 3; A.
j. Mullan, Wallace, Idaho; H. A.
Treadwell, Mabton. Wash; Nora N.
Gilbert of Seattle; Walter John
son, Nelson; Emil Schaffner,
Evansville, ILL.,, and Albert H.
Squires of Valleyford.


Attorneys Hoffman and Bailey,
before Judge Huneke of the SUI
perior court, have contested the
right of the county clerk to charge
$500 for filing dismissals.


Judgment was yesterday rem
dered against the Great Northern
railway by Judge J. d. Hinkle in
the superior court in favor of Mar
garet Rice and five children for
$1,000, she being the widow of
former employee of the company
who was killed in the Hillyard
yards while working under an en-
CINE which was bumped into by an
other locomotive.
